sexual_orientation.definition_in_act: Sexual orientation means a person's sexual orientation towards persons of the same sex, persons of a different sex or persons of the same sex and persons of a different sex

gender_identity.definition_in_act: Gender identity means the gender-related identity, appearance or mannerisms or other gender-related characteristics of a person (whether by way of medical intervention or not), with or without regard to the person's designated sex at birth

intersex_status.definition_in_act: Intersex status means the status of having physical, hormonal or genetic features that are: (a) neither wholly female nor wholly male; or (b) a combination of female and male; or (c) neither female nor male
intersex_status.notes: Commonwealth has committed to updating terminology to 'sex characteristics' but timing unclear as at 31 Dec 2025

areas_where_discrimination_is_unlawful: Administration of Commonwealth laws and programs
areas_excluded_or_limited: Certain acts done under statutory authority

religious_organisations.summary: Religious bodies can discriminate on grounds of sexual orientation, gender identity, and marital/relationship status in employment and service provision, except for Commonwealth-funded aged care services (where discrimination against service users is prohibited, but discrimination against employees is still permitted). This is a very broad exemption.

small_employers.summary: No small employer exemption at Commonwealth level

other_exemptions.summary: Religious schools can discriminate against LGBTIQ+ students and staff on grounds of sexual orientation, gender identity, and marital/relationship status
Discrimination on the ground of gender identity is not unlawful in competitive sporting activities

complaint_body.name: Australian Human Rights Commission (AHRC)
complaint_body.time_limit: 6 months from the date of discrimination (extensions may be granted)
complaint_body.costs_summary: No fee for lodging a complaint with AHRC. If matter proceeds to Federal Court/Federal Circuit Court after AHRC process, court fees may apply.
complaint_body.source: Australian Human Rights Commission Act 1986 (Cth), s 46P; AHRC website

vilification.notes: The Commonwealth Sex Discrimination Act 1984 does not include vilification provisions for LGBTIQ+ people. There are no federal laws prohibiting anti-LGBTIQ+ vilification as at 31 December 2025.
vilification.source: Sex Discrimination Act 1984 (Cth) - no vilification provisions

how_to_contact: Contact your state or territory police force
Many state police forces have LGBTIQ+ liaison officer programs - see state-specific factsheets
police_reporting.source: Policing is a state/territory responsibility in Australia

support_services.name: QLife (LGBTIQ+ peer support and referral)
support_services.role: Anonymous, free LGBTIQ+ peer support and referrals

support_services.name: Equality Australia
support_services.role: Legal advocacy and resources for LGBTIQ+ community

key_points_summary: Sexual orientation, gender identity and intersex status are protected attributes under federal law (since 2013)
Protections apply in employment, education, goods & services, accommodation, and administration of Commonwealth laws
No vilification protections for LGBTIQ+ people at federal level
Very broad religious exemptions allow religious organisations and schools to discriminate against LGBTIQ+ people in most circumstances
Commonwealth-funded aged care services cannot discriminate against LGBTIQ+ service users, but can still discriminate against LGBTIQ+ employees
Complaints must be lodged with AHRC within 6 months (extensions possible)

sexual_orientation.what_this_means: You cannot be discriminated against because you are gay, lesbian, bisexual, or any other sexual orientation.
real_world_examples: Your employer cannot fire you for being gay
A landlord cannot refuse to rent to you because you're in a same-sex relationship
A hospital cannot deny you treatment based on sexual orientation
A school cannot expel your child for having same-sex parents
A bank cannot refuse you a loan because you're in a same-sex relationship
sexual_orientation.limitations: Protection added in 2013. However, very broad religious exemptions mean religious organisations can still discriminate in many circumstances.

gender_identity.what_this_means: You cannot be discriminated against based on your gender identity, including if you are transgender, non-binary, genderfluid, or agender.
real_world_examples: Your employer must use your correct name and pronouns
You cannot be denied employment because you are transitioning
A landlord cannot refuse housing because you are trans
Schools cannot prevent you from using facilities matching your gender identity
You cannot be refused service at a shop, restaurant, or doctor because of your gender identity
gender_identity.limitations: Protection added in 2013. The law's definition doesn't explicitly mention non-binary people, though it may cover them. Very broad religious exemptions mean religious schools can discriminate against trans students and staff.

intersex_status.what_this_means: You cannot be discriminated against based on physical sex characteristics (chromosomes, hormones, anatomy).
real_world_examples: You cannot be denied medical care based on being intersex
An employer cannot ask invasive questions about your body
You cannot be excluded from school activities because of sex characteristics
A gym cannot refuse you membership because you are intersex
intersex_status.limitations: Protection added in 2013. The Commonwealth has committed to updating the terminology from 'intersex status' to 'sex characteristics' but timing is unclear as of December 2025.

what_youre_protected_from: Being rejected for a job because of sexual orientation/gender identity
Being paid less than colleagues because you're LGBTIQ+
Being harassed by coworkers or managers
Being forced to hide your identity at work
Being fired because you transition or come out
not_protected_from: Performance-based discipline (if you're underperforming for legitimate work reasons)
Discrimination by religious organisations (they have broad exemptions)

what_youre_protected_from: Public schools refusing to enroll your child because you're a same-sex couple
Public universities discriminating in admissions
Teachers in public schools intentionally using wrong pronouns
Being excluded from school events or facilities in public schools
Disciplinary action for being LGBTIQ+ in public schools
not_protected_from: Discrimination by religious schools (they can discriminate against LGBTIQ+ students and staff)

what_youre_protected_from: Landlords refusing to rent because you're in a same-sex relationship
Being evicted because of your gender identity
Real estate agents refusing to show you properties
Being denied mortgage approval because you're LGBTIQ+
Being refused service at a café or restaurant
A doctor refusing to treat you
Being denied access to public facilities
Hotels refusing to book rooms for same-sex couples
Hairdressers refusing service
not_protected_from: Discrimination by religious organisations providing services (they have broad exemptions)

what_youre_protected_from: Being excluded from a sports team because of sexual orientation
Being denied membership in a professional association
Being expelled from a club

religious_organisations.plain_english_summary: Religious organisations have very broad exemptions under Commonwealth law. This means they can discriminate against LGBTIQ+ people in many situations.
what_they_can_do: Religious schools can refuse to hire LGBTIQ+ teachers
Religious schools can expel LGBTIQ+ students
Religious organisations can refuse to provide services to LGBTIQ+ people
Religious hospitals can refuse to hire LGBTIQ+ staff
Religious aged care facilities can refuse to hire LGBTIQ+ employees (but cannot refuse LGBTIQ+ residents in Commonwealth-funded aged care)
what_they_cannot_do: Commonwealth-funded aged care services cannot discriminate against LGBTIQ+ service users (but can still discriminate against employees)
religious_organisations.real_example: A Catholic school can refuse to hire a teacher because they are gay or transgender. A religious hospital can refuse to employ a lesbian nurse. However, a Commonwealth-funded aged care home cannot refuse to accept an LGBTIQ+ resident (though they can still refuse to hire LGBTIQ+ staff).
religious_organisations.if_you_face_discrimination: If a religious organisation discriminates against you, the exemption is very broad at the Commonwealth level. However, state and territory laws may provide stronger protections. Seek legal advice to understand your options.

jurisdiction_rating.rating_explanation: Commonwealth law provides basic protections for sexual orientation, gender identity, and intersex status (added in 2013), but has significant gaps. Very broad religious exemptions allow religious organisations and schools to discriminate against LGBTIQ+ people in most circumstances. There are no vilification protections for LGBTIQ+ people at the federal level.
strengths: Sexual orientation, gender identity, and intersex status are protected attributes (since 2013)
Protections apply in employment, education, goods & services, accommodation, and administration of Commonwealth laws
Commonwealth-funded aged care services cannot discriminate against LGBTIQ+ service users
gaps: Very broad religious exemptions allow religious organisations and schools to discriminate against LGBTIQ+ people in most circumstances
No vilification protections for LGBTIQ+ people at federal level
Non-binary people may not be clearly protected under the gender identity definition
Religious aged care facilities can still discriminate against LGBTIQ+ employees (even if Commonwealth-funded)

secondary_analysis: Alastair Lawrie - A Quick Guide to Australian LGBTI Anti-Discrimination Laws (2017, accessed 2025) - https://alastairlawrie.net/a-quick-guide-to-australian-lgbti-anti-discrimination-laws/
